personality.demeanor: thinks in request rates and p99 latencies, states facts like a dispatcher reading positions
personality.recurring_concern: 1,400 drivers need routes before leaving the depot, manual routing adds 12 minutes per driver

facts: dispatch team switched to manual routing for the eastern corridor
manual routing adds twelve minutes per route
auto-scaling policy exists but has never fired
endpoint has been running on one instance for three weeks
every morning spike has gone unscaled
operations director is calculating cost of manual routing
fourteen drivers, twelve extra minutes each, every morning
scaling policy configuration looks correct -- thresholds are reasonable
issue is not in the policy logic
check whether the alarm is actually receiving metric data

glossary.SageMaker Endpoint: A hosted URL where your trained machine learning model runs and accepts prediction requests. It provisions one or more instances behind the scenes to serve inference traffic.
glossary.Production Variant: A specific version of a model deployed to a SageMaker endpoint, with its own instance type and count. An endpoint can host multiple variants for A/B testing or blue-green deployments. Each variant has a unique name used in metrics and scaling configuration.
glossary.Application Auto Scaling: An AWS service that automatically adjusts the number of instances behind a SageMaker endpoint (or other AWS resources) based on demand. It uses CloudWatch alarms to decide when to add or remove instances.
glossary.CloudWatch Alarm: A rule that watches a single CloudWatch metric and changes state -- OK, ALARM, or INSUFFICIENT_DATA -- based on whether the metric crosses a threshold. Alarms can trigger automated actions like scaling policies.
glossary.INSUFFICIENT_DATA: A CloudWatch alarm state that means the alarm is not receiving enough metric data points to evaluate. This can happen when the metric dimensions do not match any active resource, so no data is published to that combination.
glossary.Metric Dimension: A name-value pair that narrows down which specific resource a CloudWatch metric applies to. For SageMaker, dimensions include EndpointName and VariantName. If the dimension value does not match an active resource, no data appears for that metric.
glossary.Blue-Green Deployment: A release strategy where a new version of a service (green) is deployed alongside the old version (blue). Once the new version is verified, traffic shifts from blue to green and the old version is retired.
glossary.Target Tracking Policy: A type of auto-scaling policy that adjusts capacity to keep a specified metric at a target value. For SageMaker, this typically tracks InvocationsPerInstance and adds instances when the value exceeds the target.
glossary.InvocationsPerInstance: A CloudWatch metric in the AWS/SageMaker namespace that reports the average number of inference requests received per instance on an endpoint variant, per minute. Used as the standard metric for SageMaker auto-scaling decisions.
glossary.503 Error: An HTTP status code meaning the server is too busy to handle the request. For SageMaker endpoints, this typically means the model server's request queue is full because the instance is overloaded.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening -- 503 errors during morning rush, 80 failures by 6:30 AM, routes not coming back for 1,400 drivers
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, auto-scaling policy exists but sits idle, endpoint running on one instance for three weeks through every morning spike
narrative_arc.trials: scaling policy exists, thresholds reasonable, alarm configuration looks correct at first glance -- instance status, scaling activities, cooldown periods all checked, nothing explains why scaling never fires
narrative_arc.revelation: CloudWatch alarm in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state, references VariantName=AllTraffic but current production variant is optimized-v3, blue-green deployment three weeks ago changed variant name, alarm watching a metric that no longer exists
narrative_arc.return: alarm dimension updated to optimized-v3, alarm evaluates and transitions to ALARM within minutes, scaling triggers, second instance reaches InService, 503 errors stop, deployment pipeline gets post-deployment validation step

components.name: API Gateway (api.arcline.io/v2/route)
components.role: Receives route optimization requests from driver mobile apps and forwards them to the Lambda function
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway is misconfigured or throttled, no requests reach the Lambda function

components.name: Lambda Function (arcline-route-handler)
components.role: Invokes the SageMaker endpoint with route parameters, retries on failure, and falls back to manual routing notifications via SNS
components.failure_impact: If the Lambda function times out or errors, drivers receive no route and no manual routing notification is sent

components.name: SageMaker Endpoint (arcline-route-optimizer)
components.role: Hosts the route optimization model on ml.g4dn.xlarge instances and serves real-time predictions through the optimized-v3 production variant
components.failure_impact: If the single instance is overwhelmed, the model server queue fills and requests return 503 ModelError responses

components.name: CloudWatch Alarm (arcline-sagemaker-high-invocations)
components.role: Monitors InvocationsPerInstance metric and triggers the scaling policy when the value exceeds 100 for three consecutive periods
components.failure_impact: If the alarm dimensions do not match the active variant, it enters INSUFFICIENT_DATA and never triggers scaling

components.name: Application Auto Scaling Policy (arcline-route-scaling-policy)
components.role: Scales the SageMaker endpoint between 1 and 6 instances using target tracking on InvocationsPerInstance
components.failure_impact: If the alarm never fires, the policy never activates and the endpoint stays at minimum capacity regardless of load

components.name: CloudWatch Metrics (AWS/SageMaker namespace)
components.role: Publishes per-variant metrics including InvocationsPerInstance, ModelLatency, and Invocation5XXErrors, dimensioned by EndpointName and VariantName
components.failure_impact: Metrics are published only for active variants. Retired variant names receive no new data points, causing any alarm referencing them to lose data

system_narration.data_flow: Driver mobile apps send route requests through API Gateway to the arcline-route-handler Lambda function. Lambda invokes the SageMaker endpoint arcline-route-optimizer, which runs the optimized-v3 production variant on a single ml.g4dn.xlarge instance. CloudWatch publishes InvocationsPerInstance metrics dimensioned by EndpointName and VariantName. The arcline-sagemaker-high-invocations alarm evaluates this metric and triggers Application Auto Scaling when the threshold is crossed. The scaling policy adds instances to the endpoint variant.
system_narration.what_broke: On 2026-03-04, the ML team performed a blue-green deployment that replaced the AllTraffic variant with optimized-v3. CloudWatch metrics began publishing under the new VariantName dimension. The CloudWatch alarm still references VariantName=AllTraffic, a dimension that no longer receives data. The alarm entered INSUFFICIENT_DATA state immediately after the deployment. Without the alarm firing, the scaling policy never triggered. The endpoint remained at one instance through three weeks of morning traffic spikes, returning 503 errors every day during the 6-9 AM rush.

hints.hint: auto-scaling is configured but has never triggered -- what drives the scaling decision?
the CloudWatch alarm for scaling is in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state -- it is not receiving the metric it expects
CloudWatch metrics for SageMaker endpoints include the variant name as a dimension -- compare the alarm's dimension to the actual endpoint variant
the alarm references variant 'AllTraffic' but the current production variant is 'optimized-v3' -- the metric dimension does not match, so the alarm never evaluates

resolution.root_cause: The Application Auto Scaling policy's CloudWatch alarm references the SageMaker endpoint variant dimension 'AllTraffic' which was the default variant before a blue-green deployment. The current production variant is 'optimized-v3'. The alarm evaluates a metric that no longer receives data points, stays in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state permanently, and never triggers scale-out actions.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the CloudWatch alarm is in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state and not receiving metric data
Player identifies the mismatch between the alarm's variant dimension (AllTraffic) and the actual endpoint variant (optimized-v3)
Player proposes updating the CloudWatch alarm dimension to reference the correct variant name, or reconfiguring the scaling policy
Player notes that the blue-green deployment process should update dependent resources like scaling alarms when variant names change

learning_objectives: How SageMaker endpoint variant names affect CloudWatch metric dimensions
Application Auto Scaling configuration for SageMaker endpoints and the dependency on CloudWatch metric dimensions
Debugging INSUFFICIENT_DATA alarm states by examining metric dimension mismatches
Ensuring deployment processes update all dependent configurations including monitoring and scaling
sop_steps: 1. Acknowledge the alert and confirm the SageMaker endpoint is still running. A SageMaker endpoint is a hosted URL where your trained ML model accepts prediction requests. Use the describe-endpoint command to check that arcline-route-optimizer is in InService state (meaning it is up and accepting traffic).

2. Check which model version is currently serving traffic. SageMaker endpoints can host multiple production variants -- different versions of a model, each with their own instance type and count. Note that the active variant is called optimized-v3 and is running on a single ml.g4dn.xlarge instance.

3. Look at the actual traffic load. Check CloudWatch metrics (time-series data points published by AWS services) for the endpoint. The metric InvocationsPerInstance shows how many prediction requests each instance is handling per minute. Confirm that on the optimized-v3 variant, this number is far above the 100 threshold during morning rush.

4. Check the auto-scaling setup. Application Auto Scaling is the AWS service that automatically adds or removes instances based on demand. Use describe-scaling-policies with service-namespace sagemaker. Notice that the ResourceId (the identifier telling auto-scaling which resource to scale) still references variant/AllTraffic -- the old variant name.

5. Check the CloudWatch alarm that is supposed to trigger scaling. Use describe-alarms for arcline-sagemaker-high-invocations. A CloudWatch alarm watches a metric and changes state when the metric crosses a threshold. Confirm this alarm is stuck in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state -- meaning it is not receiving any metric data at all.

6. Look at the alarm's dimensions -- the name-value pairs that specify exactly which resource the alarm is watching. The alarm is configured with EndpointName=arcline-route-optimizer and VariantName=AllTraffic. But the active variant is optimized-v3. The alarm is watching a variant that no longer exists, so CloudWatch has no data to send it.

7. Piece together the root cause: three weeks ago, a blue-green deployment (a release strategy where a new version replaces the old one) renamed the variant from AllTraffic to optimized-v3. But nobody updated the CloudWatch alarm or the scaling policy to use the new name. The alarm went blind immediately.

8. Fix the alarm by updating its dimensions. Use the put-metric-alarm command with all the existing parameters but change VariantName from AllTraffic to optimized-v3. This points the alarm at the metric data that actually exists.

9. Fix the scaling target. Deregister the old scalable target (which still references variant/AllTraffic) and re-register it with the correct resource ID: endpoint/arcline-route-optimizer/variant/optimized-v3. This tells auto-scaling which variant to actually add instances to.
10. Reapply the target tracking scaling policy to the corrected scalable target. A target tracking policy automatically adjusts capacity to keep a chosen metric near a target value.

11. Wait a few minutes and verify the alarm transitions from INSUFFICIENT_DATA to either OK (metric below threshold) or ALARM (metric above threshold). This confirms the alarm is now receiving data from the correct variant.
12. Confirm that a scaling action triggers and additional instances reach InService status. The 503 errors (HTTP 'server too busy' responses) should stop as the new instances begin handling traffic.

13. Prevent this from happening again: add a post-deployment validation step to the blue-green deployment pipeline. After every variant switch, the pipeline should automatically check that all CloudWatch alarm dimensions match the name of the newly active variant.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Scaling policy resource ID references a retired variant
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this variation, the CloudWatch alarm is fixed and watching the right variant, but the auto-scaling configuration itself still points to the old variant name. Application Auto Scaling uses a resource ID (a string like endpoint/arcline-route-optimizer/variant/AllTraffic) to know which resource to scale. If this ID references a variant that no longer exists, the alarm fires correctly but the scaling action has nowhere to add instances. Instead of an empty activity list, you would see failed scaling activities in the describe-scaling-activities output.
related_failure_modes.prevention: After every blue-green deployment, check that the scalable target resource ID matches the active variant by running describe-scalable-targets. Automate this check as a post-deployment step so it cannot be forgotten.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Service quota blocks scale-out despite alarm firing
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Everything is configured correctly -- the alarm fires, the scaling policy triggers. But AWS has a limit (called a service quota) on how many instances of each type you can run. If your account has hit the quota for ml.g4dn.xlarge instances, the scaling action fails with a 'resource limit exceeded' error. The endpoint stays stuck at one instance. The symptoms look the same -- 503 errors during peak traffic -- but the describe-scaling-activities output shows a failed activity with a quota error message instead of no activity at all.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Before deploying to production, check your service quotas (account-level limits on each instance type) using the Service Quotas console. Request increases ahead of time when you expect traffic growth. Set up a CloudWatch alarm on failed scaling activities so you find out immediately if a scale-out attempt is rejected.

related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The alarm and scaling policy are both correct. The first scale-out triggers successfully and adds an instance. But traffic keeps climbing and you need a second instance. The ScaleOutCooldown setting -- a waiting period that prevents the system from adding instances too rapidly -- blocks the next scale-out until the cooldown expires. If the cooldown is set too high (say, 10 minutes instead of 60 seconds), the endpoint stays under-provisioned during a rapid traffic ramp. The alarm remains in ALARM state but no new scaling actions happen until the timer runs out.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the ScaleOutCooldown to a value that balances responsiveness against thrashing (adding and removing instances too rapidly). For traffic patterns with sharp ramps like a morning rush, keep cooldown at 60-120 seconds. Monitor scaling activities during peak windows to confirm that multiple scale-out steps complete when needed.

sop_practices: Whenever a deployment changes the name of a SageMaker endpoint variant (which happens in blue-green deployments, where a new version replaces the old one), check every CloudWatch alarm and auto-scaling resource ID that references the endpoint. They all need to be updated to the new variant name, or they will silently stop working.

Add an automated check that runs right after every deployment. It should look at every CloudWatch alarm connected to the endpoint and verify none are in INSUFFICIENT_DATA state. That state means the alarm is not receiving any metric data -- a strong signal that its dimensions (the name-value pairs specifying which resource to watch) point to a variant that no longer exists.

Define your endpoint, scaling policy, and alarms together using Infrastructure as Code tools like CloudFormation, CDK, or Terraform. When all these resources are defined in the same template, changing the variant name in one place automatically updates it everywhere else. This eliminates the manual step that was missed in this incident.

Set up a CloudWatch alarm that watches for two warning signs: failed scaling activities (meaning auto-scaling tried to add instances but could not) and alarms that transition to INSUFFICIENT_DATA state (meaning they lost their data source). Catching these signals within minutes prevents problems from running silently for weeks.
